title: NuGet.org API keys should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: NuGet.org API key detected (oy2 prefix). NuGet API keys provide authentication to publish and manage packages on NuGet.org. Exposing these keys allows attackers to publish malicious package versions, delete packages, hijack package names, inject malware into the .NET ecosystem, and compromise downstream applications. Rotate keys immediately and store securely in secret management systems.

NuGet API key detected in variable assignment. NuGet API keys authenticate package publishing and management operations on NuGet.org. Exposing these keys enables attackers to publish malicious packages, modify or delete existing packages, compromise package metadata, and launch supply chain attacks affecting .NET applications. Use secure credential storage.

NuGet package push command with hardcoded API key detected. The 'nuget push' command with -ApiKey parameter contains credentials for publishing packages. Hardcoded API keys in scripts or code allow attackers to publish malicious package versions, hijack package names, and compromise the .NET package supply chain. Use environment variables or secure credential providers.

Dotnet NuGet push command with hardcoded API key detected. The 'dotnet nuget push' command with -k/--api-key parameter contains package publishing credentials. Exposing these keys allows unauthorized package publishing, package hijacking, malicious version injection, and supply chain attacks on .NET applications. Store API keys in environment variables or secure vaults.

Generic long authentication token detected in NuGet context. Long alphanumeric tokens in NuGet-related code often represent API keys for package registry operations. Exposing these tokens allows unauthorized package publishing, package deletion, metadata modification, and supply chain attacks on the .NET ecosystem. Verify token type and rotate immediately.
